author: @bubbaDOOM1
text:
1986, schools out. My friends and I aren't doing something stupid, like playing with gunpowder, or fireworks, or setting up booby traps in a friend of mines parents shed. We used that as our headquarters. 
4:30 pm my ass was in front of the TV watching He-Man.

replies.author: @publicprofilename4273
replies.text: Don't forget jumping our bikes off of pallet ramps over a line of our friends. Gen z be like "why'd your parents let you do that?" Umm what? Parents? Oh, I remember eating dinner with them a few times now and then, but they were at work from 1976 to 1998, so yeah...

replies.author: @WickedServantEbay
replies.text:
⁠@publicprofilename4273Gen X 'free range' child myself
I see children differently than boomers saw us
Not competition, not baggage, not bugs
Children can be a better version of us, upgraded software and hardware, people who are a pleasure to be around (my 3 are)
I cant fathom how one could look down at their child and hate it

author: @maccatt7274
text: AZ im your age so, OG Star Wars figures. My dad was going through a good patch work wish but meant a lot of travel and days away from home. To compensate to sons 5 and 7 ish he would bring use back a Starwars figure each. My older brother wanted a different figure each time, me I wanted more storm troopers, I was building an army!

author: @The-Mstr-Pook
text:
Originally Prince Adam was supposed to be a kid and He-Man was an adult. so no one would know they were the same person. but film nation had such megre, small budget, that it was easier to use the same animation profiles and just changed the skin color. which is why they had that dopleganga look.

This was corrected in the 2000s reboot where they actually were more lore accurate with the characters
